coredata.dc:title: Application of fluorescent carbon quantum dots functional modified carboxymethyl cellulose film in toluene gas specific detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tengda
Han, Liang
Jia, Benzhen
Li, Qing
Huang, Xiaoyu
Dong, Hongyu
Ren, Hongwei
Zhang, Jinfeng
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing consumption of organic solvents in chemical and pharmaceutical industries, the environment pollution of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) has become an urgent problem. Therefore, the rapid-visual detection method is of great significance in the analysis of VOCs. Based on the fluorescence quenching/enhancement mechanism of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with the help of carboxymethyl cellulose membrane with porous and large specific surface area structure, a series of green CQDs@carboxymethyl cellulose composite film (CQDs@CMC composite film) was prepared in this study. In the typical-targeted pollutants (toluene) detection application, a fluorescence spectroscopy method was established which could achieve the high sensitivity and strong specificity detection. The mainly conclusions were as follows: The fluorescence spectrometric detection method for toluene: A kind of hydrophobic Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was prepared and characterized with imidazole/betaine CQDs and porous carboxymethyl cellulose composite film as raw materials. The toluene detection performance was studied, and the recognition mechanism was explored. The results showed that toluene enhanced the fluorescence of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film. The fluorescence intensity of composite films was proportional to toluene concentration when the toluene concentration ranged from 200 to 2200 mg/L. The detection limit of toluene was 1.169 mg/L, which provides a theoretical basis for the detection of toluene by fluorescence spectrometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Toluene
Carbon quantum dots
Carboxymethyl cellulose film
Fluorescent spectrometry

Application of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film

Comparison of element percentages of CMC film and CQDs@CMC composite film.
Comparison of different methods for toluene detection.

head.ce:title: Application of fluorescent carbon quantum dots functional modified carboxymethyl cellulose film in toluene gas specific detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pollution Prevention Biotechnology Laboratory of Hebei Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing consumption of organic solvents in chemical and pharmaceutical industries, the environment pollution of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) has become an urgent problem. Therefore, the rapid-visual detection method is of great significance in the analysis of VOCs. Based on the fluorescence quenching/enhancement mechanism of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with the help of carboxymethyl cellulose membrane with porous and large specific surface area structure, a series of green CQDs@carboxymethyl cellulose composite film (CQDs@CMC composite film) was prepared in this study. In the typical-targeted pollutants (toluene) detection application, a fluorescence spectroscopy method was established which could achieve the high sensitivity and strong specificity detection. The mainly conclusions were as follows: The fluorescence spectrometric detection method for toluene: A kind of hydrophobic Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was prepared and characterized with imidazole/betaine CQDs and porous carboxymethyl cellulose composite film as raw materials. The toluene detection performance was studied, and the recognition mechanism was explored. The results showed that toluene enhanced the fluorescence of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film. The fluorescence intensity of composite films was proportional to toluene concentration when the toluene concentration ranged from 200 to 2200 mg/L. The detection limit of toluene was 1.169 mg/L, which provides a theoretical basis for the detection of toluene by fluorescence spectrometry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that supports the findings of this study are available in the supplementary material of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the consumption of organic solvents in pharmaceutical, chemical and other industries is increasing, and the generated volatile organic compounds (VOCs) have high emission intensity and high toxicity, which pose a serious threat to the ecological environment and human health [1,2]. he main components of VOCs are aromatic hydrocarbons, alkanes, esters and halogenated hydrocarbons [3]. Among them, VOCs, such as toluene, has caused serious harm to human, and is regarded as the “invisible killer” of animals and plants, which has attracted the attention of all over the world [4,5]. Toluene can form an explosive mixture with air, which can explode when the volume concentration of the mixture is low. Exposure to 200–570 mg/L toluene will seriously stimulate the upper respiratory tract, eye mucosa and nerve center of human body; and exposure to 10,000 mg/L of toluene will lead to functional decline and even death [6,7].

At present, the commonly detection methods of toluene mainly include gas chromatography–mass spectrometry, ion chromatography and so on [8–10].These analysis and detection methods need to rely on large and expensive precision equipment, and have the disadvantages of complex detection procedures, long detection period and the need for professional and technical personnel to operate, so they cannot be applied to rapid on-site detection. In chemical analysis and detection, fluorescence spectrophotometry is one of the commonly used methods for spectral detection [11,12]. Based on the interaction between fluorescent probe and the detected object, the fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence intensity would response and be changed correspondingly. It has the characteristics of fast response, high sensitivity and strong specificity [13,14]. In recent years, fluorescent carbon nano-materials have become a new type of nano-fluorescent probe because of their wide sources, environmental friendliness, stable fluorescence performance and easy functionalization [15,16]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new carbon nano-material with quasi-spherical morphology and nano-scale characteristic size [17]. It is composed of crystalline or amorphous carbon cores and rich functional groups on the surface, and has excellent photoluminescence effect [18,19]. Since blue, green and yellow fluorescence were first isolated in 2004, it has been widely concerned by researchers [20]. CQDs is widely used in the fields of fluorescent probes, biosensors, optoelectronic devices and so on because of its green, low toxicity and simple preparation method, unique optical properties, and structural designability [21–24].

At present, the mainstream CQDs luminescence mechanisms are quantum confinement effect, surface state effect and molecular state effect, which are determined by carbon nucleus state [25]. Researchers believe that the fluorescence performance of CQDs is coordinated by many mechanisms [26,27]. Experimental research shows that CQDs has bright fluorescence in solution, but concentrated CQDs liquid or solid will have serious aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), and the fluorescence intensity will be greatly reduced or even disappeared, which greatly limits the practical application of CQDs [28,29]. In 2006, Sun [30] found that the surface passivation of polyethylene glycol could make CDs have higher quantum yield and thus more emittance, which proved that the stable surface energy potential well can improve the photoluminescence effect of CDs. Therefore, researchers used the method of embedding the fluorescence center into the solid matrix to enhance the photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs, and overcome the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect by inhibiting the non-radiation energy transfer caused by π -π stacking in the conjugated region of CQDs and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) caused by the interaction between molecules [31,32], so as to realize the solid-state luminescence of CQDs.

Fluorescence spectrometry based on the change of fluorescence intensity in CQDs has overcome the shortcomings of high operating cost, complicated detection procedures and long detection period. It is found that imidazolium-based deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have high affinity for VOCs, especially for the absorption of hydrophobic toluene gas containing benzene ring structure. With CO and CO, Betaine (Bet) could behave as electron donors. It can form stable electron pairs with the electron-drawing groups on benzene rings. Lmidazole (Lmi) reacts with Bet to generate nitrogen-containing functionalized CQDs, and it can form hydrogen bonds with toluene, which increases the adsorption of toluene gas by CQDs composite film, thus achieving the effect of efficient detection of toluene gas. In this study, Lmi was used as hydrogen bond donor and Bet as hydrogen bond acceptor, Lmi/Bet DESs was prepared as CQDs precursor, and hydrophobic Lmi/Bet CQDs was prepared by one-step hot solvent method. Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC was prepared by hydrophobic modification of composite CQDs and carboxymethyl cellulose membrane. Through a series of characterization methods, the physical and chemical properties of Lmi/Bet CQDs and Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC film and the detection effect of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC film on toluene gas were explored.

Glycine betaine (C5H11NO2 99.0 %), Lmidazole (C3H4N2 99.0 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH 98 %), Benzene (C6H6 99.0 %), Propanone (CH3COCH3 99.0 %), Cyclohexane (C6H12 99.0 %), Methanol (CH3OH 99.0 %), Ethyl acetate (C4H8O2 99.0 %), Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl 37 %), Methylbenzene (C7H8), and Methane (CH4).

The size and distribution of Lmi/Bet CQDs in the solution were characterized by transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan) under 200 kV acceleration voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al K Alpha (hv = 1340 eV) in standard lens mode (Thermo Fischer, Waltham, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany) was used to characterize the surface functional groups of Lmi/Bet CQDs and Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC in the frequency range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. XRD patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD-7000, Shimadzu, Japan) with a scanning range of 20–80°. The cold field emission scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) was used to characterize Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC. Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure the fluorescence properties of materials. Steady-state / Lifetime Spectrofluorometer (FL920, EdinburghInstruments, EI, UK) was used to measure the fluorescence lifetime of Lmi/Bet CQDs and Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC.

8.16 g of Lmidazole (Lmi) and 4.68 g of glycine betaine (Gly) were put into a magnetic stirrer, and stirred at 100 °C for 2 h. The resulting homogeneous transparent liquid was the obtained Lmi/Bet natural deep eutectic solvent (Lmi/Bet NADES).

To prepare Lmi/Bet CQDs, 1.5 g of the Lmi/Bet NADES was put on a high-pressure reactor, then put into a Blast drying oven and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, yellow solution containing Lmi/Bet CQDs was obtained. The yellow solution was placed in a high-speed table centrifuge, centrifuged at 8000 rpm·s−1 for 20 min, and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain Lmi/Bet CQDs solution for later use.

0.8 g of Carboxymethyl Cellulose (CMC) and 50 mL of deionized water were put into a flask, and stirred at 62 °C for 40 min. The resulting colorless transparent liquid 50 mL and 30 mL anhydrous ethanol were mixed with ultrasonic cleaning machine and fully shaken until completely mixed. The mixed solution was poured into the cell culture dish slowly and used a blast dryer to dry the solution until it becomes a transparent film to obtain Lmi/Bet CQDs-CMC film (Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on gas selectivity and anti-interference detection of toluene with Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm gas selectivity of the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film, it was placed in a gas buffer bottle, and 2000 mg/L of toluene, benzene, methanol, ethanol, ethyl acetate, cyclohexane and acetone were injected respectively. Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was kept in the gas environment for 30 s. The fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC was detected in different gas environments. Fluorescence emission spectra of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film under different gases were plotted and the selectivity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film for toluene gas detection was analyzed.

To investigate the anti-interference of the film, the same concentration of toluene gas was mixed with benzene, methanol, ethanol, ethyl acetate, cyclohexane and acetone respectively, and injected into the buffer bottle containing Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film for 30 s. The fluorescence emission intensities of the film were detected, and the anti-interference of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film for toluene was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on gas sensitivity detection of toluene with Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC
ce:section.ce:para: Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was placed in a gas buffer bottle, and 200 mg/L、400 mg/L, 600 mg/L, 800 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, 1200 mg/L, 1400 mg/L, 1600 mg/L, 1800 mg/L, 2000 mg/L, 2200 mg/L of toluene were injected respectively. Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was kept in the gas concentration for 30 s. The fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC was detected in different toluene concentration. The sensitivity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film for toluene gas detection was analyzed.

The detection limit of detection(LOD) of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC to toluene gas is calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
LOD =
3 δ
S
In the equation: δ — Standard deviation of blank sample;

S — Fits the slope of the equation(F
0
F)/F
0 .

The particle size and distribution of Lmi/Bet CQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy. The TEM image (Fig. 1a) shows that the prepared CQDs have good dispersion, uniform particle size and spherical morphology. The particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b) shows that most of the Lmi/Bet CQDs are in the range of 12.5–17.5 nm, with an average particle size of 15.5 nm. The valence electron transition and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1.

In this study, quinine sulfate dihydrate was prepared with 0.1 mol·L−1 H2SO4 as solvent. The quantum yield of quinine sulfate dihydrate was known to be
Φ r
= 0.54. The quantum yield of CQDs was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The fluorescence quantum yield of Lmi/Bet CQDs is 5.13 % with quinine sulfate as the fluorescence reference at 330 nm excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence attenuation curve corresponds to the double exponential decay behavior after fitting the fluorescence attenuation curve [33]. According to the second-order fitting data, A
1 = 0.36615, A
2 = 0.64276, τ
1 = 2.36099 ns, τ
2 = 8.09169 ns.

The average life of Lmi/Bet CQDs is 7.83 ns as obtained from eq. (3–5). The fluorescence attenuation curve of Lmi/Bet CQDs is consistent with double exponential attenuation because the photoluminescence effect of Lmi/Bet CQDs is caused by the multiple fluorescence emission center caused by abundant defects on the surface of CQDs [34]. Therefore, the abundant functional groups on the surface of Lmi/Bet CQDs provide the basis for the specific detection of p-toluene.

The fluorescence quantum yield is calculated according to the following eq. (2):
(2)
Φ c
=
Φ r
×
F c
F r
×
A r
A c
×
η c 2
η r 2
in the equation
⁤
c — Determinand (Lmi/Bet CQDs);
⁤
r — Reference material;
⁤
Φ — Quantum yield;
⁤
F — Integral area of fluorescence emission spectrum;
⁤
A — Absorbance at excitation wavelength 360 nm；.
⁤
η — Solvent refractive index.

The fluorescence attenuation curve is fitted according to the fluorescence attenuation data. When the fluorescence attenuation curve is a 2-order fitting curve, the average fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs is calculated according to the following average lifetime calculation equation:
(3)
A 1 '
=
A 1
τ 1
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
(4)
A 2 '
=
A 2
τ 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
(5)
τ =
A 1 '
τ 1 2
+
A 2 '
τ 2 2
A 1 '
τ 1
+
A 2 '
τ 2
in the equation
⁤
τ — Fluorescence lifetime;
⁤
τ 1
— The fluorescence lifetime value of the first fit;
⁤
τ 2
— The fluorescence lifetime value of the second fit;
⁤
A — Empirical fit constant;
⁤
A
1 — First fit constant;
⁤
A
2 — Second fit constant.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface elemental composition and chemical state of Lmi/Bet CQDs. According to the characteristic peak distribution, Gaussian fitting was performed on the raw data to separate out the hidden peaks, as shown in Fig. 3 . The XPS full spectrum clearly shows six characteristic peaks: O1s (529 eV), N1s (443 eV), C1s (283 eV). It shows that the Lmi/Bet CQDs contain O, N and C elements, which are consistent with the elements contained in the raw materials for the synthesis of Lmi/Bet CQDs. The relative contents of elements were O(60.38 %), N(3.67 %) and C(35.95 %), respectively. The high-resolution spectra of the three main elements of C, O and N are further analyzed by Fig. 3a, b and c. The peaks at 283 Ev, 284.8 eV, and 286.8 eV of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be assigned to CO, C=C/C-C and C-O-C/O-C=O, respectively. The peaks at 400.8 eV, 399 eV and 397.9 eV of the N1s spectrum can be assigned to -NH+, C-N-C and C-N=C, respectively. The peaks at 529.8 eV, and 528.2 eV of the O1s spectrum can be assigned to CO, and NO, respectively. Among them, C-O-C, O-C=O, CO and C-N-C on the surface of Lmi/Bet CQDs are hydrophobic functional groups, which enhance the hydrophobicity of Lmi/Bet CQDs. The CO, -OH, -NH+ groups can react with toluene, which greatly improves the sensitivity of Lmi/Bet CQDs for toluene gas detection.

To explore the effect of pH value on the fluorescence stability of Lmi/Bet CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , when the Lmi/Bet CQDs system is in neutral condition (pH = 7), the fluorescence intensity is the highest, the highest is 5742 a.u. When the pH of Lmi/Bet CQDs system gradually becomes acidic or alkaline, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs system shows a downward trend. When pH = 14, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs was 5034 a.u., which decreased by 12.3 %. In general, when the pH in the system is strong acid or strong base, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs decreases slightly, but the overall change is not large. Therefore, Lmi/Bet CQDs has good stability at pH = 1–14.

As shown in Fig. 5 , when the temperature is 20 °C, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs is the highest, and when the temperature exceeds 20 °C, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs decreases with the increase of temperature, but the overall fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs does not change much. Therefore, under the condition of temperature of 10–50 °C, the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDS decreases. Temperature has a slight and negligible effect on the stability of Lmi/Bet CQDs.

The influence of placement time on the stability of Lmi/Bet CQDs was explored in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs was slightly reduced by placement time, but remained basically stable. Therefore, the prepared Lmi/Bet CQDS had good temporal stability.

As displayed in Fig. 7 , SEM images of the CMC film and Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC thin film show that Lmi/Bet CQDs is successfully loaded and distributed on the CMC thin film. The embedding of Lmi/Bet CQDs in the CMC thin film structure reduces the accumulation of CQDs, increases the distance between CQDs, effectively suppresses the π-π interaction in the aggregated state, and reduces the energy transfer loss. Solid luminescence of Lmi/Bet CQDs was realized by surface passivation of CMC film.

The dispersion loading of elements in Lmi/Bet CQDs on CMC film was characterized by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The EDS (Fig. S4a) and Mapping spectra (Fig. S4b, c, d) of CMC film are shown in Fig. S4, and it can be seen that carbon (C), oxygen (O), and sodium (Na) exist on CMC film.

Fig. 8 , EDS spectrum is used to characterize the element distribution of CQDs@CMC film. Compared with the original CMC film (Fig. S4), the total spectrum of elements in CQDs@CMC film (Fig. 8a), there are carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na) and nitrogen (N) in the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film, and the distribution Mapping of elements is shown as Fig. 8(b), (c), (d) and (e) respectively, from which it can be seen that the distribution of all elements is uniform.

Comprising the element percentages of CMC film and CQDs@CMC composite film (Table 1 ), it could be observed that the atoms of C, O and Na elements in CMC thin films account for 46.27 %, 48.57 % and 5.16 %. The carbon atoms of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film account for 41.73 %, oxygen, sodium and nitrogen atoms account for 35.88 %, 3.59 % and 18.80 %. The content and kinds of chemical elements on the two films all are changed greatly. Especially the element of N, which belongs to CQDs, is found in the CQDs@CMC composite film, and the atomic percent accounts for 18.80 %, which proves that Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is successfully prepared.

The fluorescence emission spectra of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film were determined by excitation wavelength 260–350 nm (EX Slit 2.5–15, the optimized EX Slit 2.5; EX Slit 2.5-20, the optimized EX Slit 5). As displayed in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence emission curves show different fluorescence intensity and emission peak positions at different excitation wavelengths, indicating that Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film have strong excitation wavelength dependence, which accords with the photoluminescence effect of fluorescence products. Lmi/Bet CQDs is emitted at 390 nm. Comparing the fluorescence emission wavelength of Lmi/Bet CQDs 330 nm (Fig. S2), the emission wavelength of the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is 280 nm, and it is obviously blue-shifted. Since CMC thin film has no fluorescence effect, the blue shift of emission wavelength after Lmi/Bet CQDs composite may be caused by the interaction between Lmi/Bet CQDs and CMC thin film, which changes the fluorescence effect of CQDs. Due to the dominant role of surface state luminescence, carboxymethyl cellulose membrane effectively reduces the aggregation phenomenon of CQDs, increases the distance between CQDs, thereby affecting the surface state energy potential well of CQDs and increasing the fluorescence intensity. Thus, the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was successfully prepared, and the solid-state luminescence of Lmi/Bet CQDs was realized.

The infrared spectral lines of CMC thin film (black line) and Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC thin film (red line) were shown in Fig. 10 . The wide absorption peak at 3185 cm−1 is caused by N-H/O-H stretching vibration. The characteristic peaks at 1589 cm−1, 1411 cm−1, 1319 cm−1and 1033 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of CO, C=C/C-H, C-N-C and C-O-C, respectively.

By comparing the infrared spectra of Lmi/Bet CQDs, it is found that the position of the characteristic peak of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is basically unchanged, which indicates that the composite of Lmi/Bet CQDs and CMC film does not change the main functional groups on the surface. By comparing the changes of infrared spectra of CMC thin films before and after the addition of Lmi/Bet CQDs, it can be seen that the absorption intensity of characteristic peaks increases, which proves that the concentration of loaded functional groups on CMC thin films increases after the addition of Lmi/Bet CQDs. Lmi/Bet CQDs successfully bonded with CMC film through newly formed chemical bonds and hydrogen bonds generated by carbonyl and hydroxyl groups. In addition, the hydrogen bond can effectively prevent the energy loss caused by molecular rotation or vibration, so as to protect the fluorescence emission effect of Lmi/Bet CQDs on the CMC film, and realize the solid luminescence of Lmi/Bet CQDs on the CMC film.

At the excitation wavelength of 280 nm, the fluorescence quantum yield of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC thin film is 6.32 % calculated by eq. 2. The fluorescence attenuation curve of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is shown in Fig. 11 . Through fitting calculation, the average life of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is 8.9 ns. The fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of Lmi/Bet CQDs were respectively compared. After Lmi/Bet CQDs was combined with carboxymethyl cellulose membrane, the fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of Lmi/Bet CQDs increased. Therefore, the fluorescence properties of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film are better than that of Lmi/Bet CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC thin films respectively placed in toluene, benzene, ethyl acetate, methanol, ethanol, cyclohexane, methane, and acetone with a concentration of 2000 mg/L for 30 s at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. When the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film was exposed to 2000 mg/L toluene gas, the fluorescence intensity increased significantly, and other VOCs gases made the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film quenched to different degrees, with the highest quenching rate of 10.1 %.

The fluorescence enhancement rate (FF
0 )/F
0 of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film under different VOCs gases was shown in Fig. 12b (F
0 is the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film measured at λex = 280 nm; F is the fluorescence intensity measured by Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film under different VOCs gases). After absorption of different VOCs, the value of (F
F
0 )/F
0 of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is 0.34404, which is greatly changed. The fluorescence enhancement effect of toluene gas on the composite film is obviously different from the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of ethanol, cyclohexane, methane, acetone, et al.. Therefore, Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film have the best selectivity for toluene gas when placed under different VOCs.

The anti-interference property is also very important for the reliability of detection. In the experiment, the possible inter-influencing gases such as xylene, carbinol, and ethyl acetate, et al. were mixed with the target toluene as coexisting substants, and the PL intensities of were determined. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs+toluene and CQDs+toluene+inter-influencing gases was not changed obviously, and the foreign substants had little effect on the results of toluene detection (Fig. 13 ).

As can be seen from Fig. 14a, the peak fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film gradually increases with the increase of toluene gas concentration. According to the fluorescence enhancement change rate (FF
0 )/F
0 , as shown in Fig. 14b, fluorescence enhancement change rate (FF
0 )/F
0 presents a linear relationship between toluene gas concentration and fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite films when toluene gas concentration ranges from 200 to 2200 mg/L.

The fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite films is linearly related to toluene gas concentration. The fitting eq. (FF
0 )/F
0 = 2.924× + 0.1513, R2 = 0.9889. The standard deviation δ of the blank sample is 0.3855. Calculated by Eq. (1), the detection limit LOD of toluene gas in Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is 1.169 ppm.

Conventional detection methods for toluene include gas chromatography (GC) [35], ultraviolet spectrophotometry (UV) [36], photoionization detector (PID) [37], Electrochemistry [38] and infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) [39]. Compared with the traditional detection methods of toluene (Table 2 ), the CQDs fluorescence detection method of toluene in this study merits the detection reliability, green-easy operation, and low cost. It would have a promising application in the detection of VOCs.

In this experiment, hydrophobic Lmi/Bet CQDs was combined with porous CMC film by hydrogen bonding and chemical bonding to realize hydrophobic modification of CMC film. As shown in Fig. 15 , the fluorescence intensity of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC thin film increased obviously when it was exposed in toluene gas. The enhanced fluorescence reason might be due to the conjugation of toluene and Lmi/Bet CQDs. The numbers of functional groups (-NH2, -NO2, and C=O-) on Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC film could provide active sites [40]. They could react with the methyl and benzene ring on toluene to form by hydrogen bond. Thus, conjugate effect between NH2, -NO2, and C=O- and toluene was generated when the hydrophobic interaction occurred between Lmi/Bet CQDs and toluene. The bond energy of chromophore groups of -C=NH, -C···H···NO2, and C=O-C in the system were all increased accordingly [41], making the Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC film produce stronger fluorescence emission..

In this work, Lmi/Bet NADESs was used as the precursor to prepare Lmi/Bet CQDs by one-step thermal solvent method. Lmi/Bet CQDs has good fluorescence stability in storage time, ambient temperature and pH of the system. The CMC solution was mixed with Lmi/Bet CQDs by ultrasonic vibration and dried to obtain Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC. Based on the fluorescence quenching/ enhancement mechanism of VOCs by CQDs, this study analyzed the interaction principle between CQDs and CMC film, explored the recognition mechanism of CMC film to toluene gas. At the same time, a fluorescence spectrometry with high sensitivity and strong specificity was established. The fluorescence effect of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC on toluene was detected by fluorescence spectrometry. The change rate of fluorescence enhancement of Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film is linear with the concentration of toluene gas in the range of 200–2200 mg/L. The results show that Lmi/Bet CQDs@CMC composite film can detect toluene gas, which provides a reliable theoretical basis for the application of fluorescence spectrometry to the actual industrial emission of toluene and the detection of toluene gas in life.

Tengda Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Liang Han: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Benzhen Jia: Visualization, Investigation. Qing Li: Validation, Investigation. Xiaoyu Huang: Supervision. Hongyu Dong: Visualization. Hongwei Ren: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jinfeng Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflict of interests，we do not have any possible conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046, E2021208033), National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Hebei University of Science and Technology Graduate Innovation Ability Training funding project (XJCXZZSS2022013), Hebei Province Graduate Student Innovation Ability Training Funding Project (CXZZSS2023091).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.134005.
